content.text: is the practice of issuing multiple tool calls simultaneously from an AI agent, rather than waiting for each tool to complete before starting the next. When an agent needs results from several independent sources — a web search, a database lookup, and a file read, for instance — it dispatches all three at once and waits only for the slowest one to finish, rather than paying the latency cost of each sequentially.

In frameworks that support it (LangChain, LangGraph, the Claude API, OpenAI function calling), parallel tool execution is often expressed as the model returning a list of tool-call objects in a single response turn. The runtime then executes all of them concurrently and feeds all the results back to the model in the next turn.

Latency is the most visible cost in an agentic system. A research agent that calls five APIs sequentially, each taking 500 ms, adds 2.5 seconds of I/O wait to every response — before the model has even had a chance to reason about the results. Users notice delays above 200 ms; above one second they feel disengaged.
Parallel execution collapses those per-tool delays. The wall-clock time shifts from the
of all tool latencies to the
of tool latencies. For independent tools, this is a structural win that no amount of prompt engineering or model speed can replicate.
wall-clock time becomes max(tool latencies) instead of sum(tool latencies). For three tools of 800 ms, 400 ms, 600 ms the saving is ~56% (800 ms vs 1,800 ms).
the agent can serve more tasks per unit time since I/O waits no longer serialize work.
network connections and CPU are kept busy concurrently rather than sitting idle while one tool responds.
gathering multiple pieces of information in one model turn reduces the total number of LLM calls, which also reduces cost.
the model can express its full information-gathering intent in one response, rather than interleaving reasoning and fetching across many turns.
if Tool B needs the output of Tool A, they cannot run in parallel. Dependency analysis — manual or automatic — is required to identify which tools are truly independent.
firing many concurrent requests to the same API can trigger rate-limit errors. Concurrency must be bounded or back-off strategies applied per API.
partial failure (some tools succeed, some fail) is harder to handle than sequential failure. The agent must decide whether to proceed with partial results or retry.
returning results from many tools simultaneously adds more tokens to the context in one step, which can exhaust context limits faster than a selective sequential approach.
tools with side effects (writes, sends, mutations) should not run in parallel unless ordering is guaranteed or idempotent. Parallel writes to the same resource can corrupt state.

When a model with tool-calling support determines it needs information from multiple independent sources, it emits several tool-call objects in a single response. Each object carries a unique call ID, a tool name, and the arguments to pass. The runtime — your code, LangChain, or an agent SDK — iterates the list and dispatches each call on its own thread, coroutine, or async task. All calls proceed concurrently. When every call has returned (or timed out), the runtime assembles the results — keyed by call ID — into a tool-result message and sends them back to the model as a batch.

The model then processes all results in one pass, which is more efficient than processing one result at a time. Internally, the model sees a context that contains: its previous response with the tool calls, then a single message containing all the tool results. It does not see the results arriving one-by-one.

attrs.alt: Sequential vs parallel tool execution: sequential sums latencies (1800ms), parallel uses only the slowest tool latency (800ms)

content.text: Use parallel tool execution when all of the following are true:
no tool's input depends on another tool's output. If Tool B needs Tool A's result, they must run sequentially.
The tools are read-only or safely concurrent:
reads, searches, and lookups are safe to parallelize. Writes to the same resource should not be parallelized unless they are idempotent and targeting different keys.
the agent genuinely needs results from more than one tool in the same reasoning step.
interactive agents, real-time pipelines, and user-facing products benefit most. Batch offline pipelines may not need the complexity.
Do not parallelize when tools have side effects that depend on each other (e.g., create a record then update it), or when the combined output would overflow your context window.
Inspect your tool graph and mark which tools share no data dependency. Only these can be safely parallelized.
Enable parallel tool calls in your model client.
(or leave it as default, which is enabled). The model will then return multiple tool-call objects in one response when appropriate.
Execute tool calls concurrently in your runtime.
When the model returns a list of tool calls, use
(JS), or a thread pool to execute them concurrently.
Collect results keyed by tool call ID.
Each tool result must carry the call ID from the model's response so the model can correlate result to call.
Return all results in one message.
message to the conversation containing all results. Do not send them one at a time.
If a tool raises an exception, catch it and return an error result for that call ID rather than letting the entire batch fail.
to cap concurrency per API, preventing rate-limit violations when many tools target the same upstream service.

import asyncio
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# --- Tool implementations ---

async def web_search(query: str) -> str:
    await asyncio.sleep(0.8)  # Simulate 800ms network I/O
    return f"Search results for: {query}"

async def read_file(path: str) -> str:
    await asyncio.sleep(0.4)  # Simulate 400ms disk I/O
    return f"File contents of {path}: ..."

async def call_api(endpoint: str) -> str:
    await asyncio.sleep(0.6)  # Simulate 600ms external API
    return f"API response from {endpoint}"

TOOL_FUNCTIONS = {
    "web_search": web_search,
    "read_file": read_file,
    "call_api": call_api,
}

# --- Parallel executor ---

async def execute_tool(tool_call) -> dict:
    """Execute one tool call and return a tool_result block."""
    fn = TOOL_FUNCTIONS[tool_call.name]
    try:
        result = await fn(**tool_call.input)
        return {
            "type": "tool_result",
            "tool_use_id": tool_call.id,
            "content": result,
        }
    except Exception as exc:
        return {
            "type": "tool_result",
            "tool_use_id": tool_call.id,
            "is_error": True,
            "content": str(exc),
        }

async def run_agent():
    tools = [
        {"name": "web_search", "description": "Search the web",
         "input_schema": {"type": "object", "properties": {"query": {"type": "string"}}, "required": ["query"]}},
        {"name": "read_file", "description": "Read a file",
         "input_schema": {"type": "object", "properties": {"path": {"type": "string"}}, "required": ["path"]}},
        {"name": "call_api", "description": "Call an external API",
         "input_schema": {"type": "object", "properties": {"endpoint": {"type": "string"}}, "required": ["endpoint"]}},
    ]

    messages = [
        {"role": "user", "content": "Search for 'AI trends', read config.json, and call /status — all at once."}
    ]

    import time
    start = time.monotonic()

    # First model turn: expects multiple tool calls
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        tools=tools,
        messages=messages,
    )

    while response.stop_reason == "tool_use":
        tool_calls = [b for b in response.content if b.type == "tool_use"]
        print(f"Model requested {len(tool_calls)} tool(s) in parallel")

        # Execute all tool calls concurrently
        results = await asyncio.gather(*[execute_tool(tc) for tc in tool_calls])

        # Append assistant message + all results in one user message
        messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response.content})
        messages.append({"role": "user", "content": list(results)})

        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=1024,
            tools=tools,
            messages=messages,
        )

    elapsed = time.monotonic() - start
    print(f"Done in {elapsed:.2f}s")
    # Final text response
    return next(b.text for b in response.content if b.type == "text")

asyncio.run(run_agent())

import asyncio
import httpx

async def execute_tools_bounded(tool_calls, max_concurrent: int = 3):
    """
    Execute tool calls with a cap on simultaneous in-flight requests.
    Essential when targeting APIs with strict rate limits.
    """
    semaphore = asyncio.Semaphore(max_concurrent)

    async def run_one(tool_call):
        async with semaphore:  # blocks until a slot is free
            return await execute_tool(tool_call)  # reuse the execute_tool from above

    return await asyncio.gather(*[run_one(tc) for tc in tool_calls])

# Usage: replace asyncio.gather(...) with this function
# results = await execute_tools_bounded(tool_calls, max_concurrent=5)


# --- Timeout guard ---

async def execute_tool_with_timeout(tool_call, timeout_s: float = 10.0) -> dict:
    """Wrap a tool call with a hard timeout so one slow tool cannot block the batch."""
    try:
        return await asyncio.wait_for(execute_tool(tool_call), timeout=timeout_s)
    except asyncio.TimeoutError:
        return {
            "type": "tool_result",
            "tool_use_id": tool_call.id,
            "is_error": True,
            "content": f"Tool '{tool_call.name}' timed out after {timeout_s}s",
        }

# LangGraph runs tool nodes in parallel when multiple tool calls share the same
# reasoning step. The ToolNode class handles this automatically.

from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from langgraph.prebuilt import create_react_agent
from langchain_core.tools import tool
import asyncio

@tool
async def web_search(query: str) -> str:
    """Search the web for information."""
    await asyncio.sleep(0.8)
    return f"Results: {query}"

@tool
async def read_document(doc_id: str) -> str:
    """Read a document by ID."""
    await asyncio.sleep(0.4)
    return f"Document {doc_id}: ..."

@tool
async def check_status(service: str) -> str:
    """Check the status of a service."""
    await asyncio.sleep(0.3)
    return f"Service {service}: healthy"

llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-opus-4-5")

# create_react_agent with an async model automatically parallelizes
# tool calls that the model emits in one response turn.
agent = create_react_agent(llm, [web_search, read_document, check_status])

async def main():
    result = await agent.ainvoke({
        "messages": [{"role": "user", "content":
            "Search for 'vector databases', read doc-42, and check status of 'api-gateway'."
        }]
    })
    print(result["messages"][-1].content)

asyncio.run(main())

A product support agent receives a ticket: "Why is my order #8821 late?". To answer, it needs three things: the order record from the database, the shipping carrier's tracking status, and the user's past support history. These are independent reads from three different systems. Sequential execution at 500 ms each takes 1.5 seconds; parallel execution takes 500 ms.
Executing tool calls sequentially even when the model returns a list.
Many tutorial implementations loop over tool calls with
for tc in tool_calls: result = await execute(tc)
— this serializes them and throws away all the latency benefit.
Sending results back one at a time.
The Claude API requires all tool results for a given assistant turn to arrive in a single
message. Sending them as separate messages causes a 400 error.
If Tool A creates a resource that Tool B depends on, running them in parallel means Tool B may run before the resource exists. Always identify the dependency graph first.
If one tool in a batch throws an exception and you propagate it, the entire
or wrap each call in try/except so failures in one tool don't abort the batch.
Unbounded parallelism can hit API rate limits or overwhelm a fragile upstream. Always add a
for any tool that calls an external service.
One hanging tool blocks the entire gather. Wrap each call with
so a single slow tool degrades gracefully rather than stalling the agent.
Build a dependency graph before implementing.
Draw out which tools depend on which. Parallelize only within independent groups. Tools in the same group can run together; groups must run sequentially.
over thread pools for I/O-bound tools. Threads add overhead and GIL contention; async is designed for concurrent I/O.
Always return results keyed by call ID.
field in each result must match the corresponding
in the model's tool-use block. Mismatches cause silent context confusion.
Record the number of parallel calls, individual latencies, and any partial failures. This data reveals which tools are bottlenecks and whether parallelism is actually being achieved.
Synthetic tests with no I/O will not reveal race conditions or partial failure modes. Run integration tests against real or simulated slow backends.
The latency improvement from parallel execution follows a straightforward relationship. If you have
For uniformly distributed latencies, the speedup scales approximately as
(n tools, n times faster). For skewed distributions where one tool dominates, the speedup approaches n minus the fraction of time spent on the slowest tool.

Scalability considerations: as the number of concurrent tool calls grows, upstream APIs may throttle more aggressively, and the aggregated result payload grows. For workloads with >20 parallel calls, consider chunking into waves of bounded size (e.g., 5 calls per wave) rather than a single unbounded gather.
Native — model returns list of tool_use blocks
Developer executes calls concurrently; SDK does not auto-dispatch
Automatic — async ToolNode parallelizes by default
Use ainvoke; sync invoke is sequential
Handles gather and result assembly internally
Parallelism is durable — crashes don't re-run completed calls
Dependent tools, simple agents, low-latency targets
Independent reads, interactive agents, multi-source queries
Repeated identical calls within a session
Predictable access patterns with high confidence
UX-sensitive: show output incrementally as each tool returns
Parallel execution introduces failure modes that do not exist in sequential execution. Address each explicitly:
so exceptions in individual tools are returned as values rather than propagated. Inspect each result and return an error
block for failed calls so the model can reason about the failure and decide whether to retry or proceed.
a burst of parallel requests can hit per-minute quotas of upstream APIs (e.g., Google Search, Slack, GitHub). Cap concurrency with a
and add exponential back-off on 429 responses.

in parallel mode, a malicious tool result cannot block the batch (since all calls proceed regardless), but it can still inject content into the model's context. Validate and sanitize all tool results before adding them to the conversation.

if a batch partially fails and you retry failed calls, ensure those tools are idempotent — running them twice must produce the same result as running them once. This is critical for tools that create or modify resources.
one unresponsive upstream can stall a gather indefinitely without a timeout. Use
per call, and implement a circuit breaker for persistently failing tools to prevent cascading slowdowns.
Parallel tool execution replaces the sum of tool latencies with the maximum — the single largest latency saving available without changing tools or models.
The Claude API supports parallel tool use natively: when the model returns multiple
blocks in one response, all results must be returned in a single
Only parallelize independent tools — tools whose inputs do not depend on another tool's outputs. Build a dependency graph first.
timeouts so partial failures degrade gracefully rather than aborting the batch.
to prevent rate-limit violations when multiple tools call the same upstream API simultaneously.
Never parallelize tools with ordering-dependent side effects — writes to the same resource, dependent mutations, or operations where sequence correctness is required.
LangGraph's async ToolNode handles parallel dispatch automatically; the Anthropic SDK requires you to implement
yourself in the tool execution loop.
